1: ὦ ἄνθρωπε πᾶς ὁ κρίνων (ō anthrōpe pas ho krinōn) — "O man, every one who judges" — the vocative ō is rhetorical, addressing a generic figure (the moralistic judge). Paul does not yet specify Jew or Gentile, though the argument progressively reveals he has the morally self-confident Jew particularly in mind by vv17-20. ἀναπολόγητος — "without defense" — the same word as Romans 1:20; the judge who condemns others on the basis of the knowledge that he himself suppresses is equally without excuse.
2: κρίμα τοῦ θεοῦ (krima tou theou) — "judgment of God" — a judicial noun (krima = the act of judging, or the verdict rendered). κατὰ ἀλήθειαν (kata alētheian) — "according to truth" — God's judgment is measured against truth (the standard he himself is), not against the self-defined moral systems of the judge; the contrast with human hypocrisy is built into the standard.
3: λογίζῃ (logizē) — "do you reckon/calculate?" — the same forensic term Paul uses in 3:28 and Romans 4:3-8 for imputation. Here it is ironic: the judge is calculating/reasoning incorrectly about divine judgment, imagining that moral posturing exempts from the verdict that comes on those who do the same things.
4:
χρηστότητος (chrēstotētos), ἀνοχῆς (anochēs), μακροθυμίας (makrothymias) — three distinct Greek terms for divine patience: (1) chrēstotēs = kindness, moral goodness in disposition; (2) anochē = forbearance, the restraint of wrath that could be exercised; (3) makrothymia = long-suffering, enduring capacity not to retaliate. Their accumulation signals that Paul is not using synonyms loosely but cataloguing the specific qualities of divine patience.

εἰς μετάνοιαν (eis metanoian) — "toward repentance" — the purpose of divine kindness is the behavioral-and-cognitive turning that metanoia denotes (meta = after, noia = mind; the after-mind, the changed perspective that produces changed conduct).
5: σκληρότητα καὶ ἀμετανόητον καρδίαν (sklērotēta kai ametanoēton kardian) — "hardness and unrepentant heart" — sklērotēs (hardness) echoes the OT hardening of Pharaoh's heart; ametanoētos (unrepentant) is Paul's only use of this adjective. The irony is that the one who calls others to repentance has an unrepentant heart. ὀργῆς (orgēs) — "wrath" — the day of wrath is a treasury being accumulated by every act of unrepentant hardness: θησαυρίζεις (thēsaurizeis) = "you are treasuring up" (present active), an active storing of future judgment.
6: ἀποδώσει ἑκάστῳ κατὰ τὰ ἔργα αὐτοῦ (apodōsei hekastō kata ta erga autou) — "he will render to each according to his works" — the future active of apodidōmi (to give back what is owed). The standard is individual works rather than group membership. Paul is not setting up a works-salvation principle that will later be qualified by grace; he is first establishing that God's judgment is impartial and individual, which is essential before revealing that none can meet the standard (Romans 3:20).
7: ζητοῦσιν δόξαν καὶ τιμὴν καὶ ἀφθαρσίαν (zētousin doxan kai timēn kai aphtharsian) — "seeking glory and honor and immortality" — the three nouns move from present experience to future reality. Aphtharsian (incorruptibility) is the eschatological dimension: the transformed body not subject to the decay of the present creation. Paul's description of eternal life (v7) is emphatically concrete and embodied.
8: ἐξ ἐριθείας (ex eritheias) — "from selfish ambition" — eritheia originally meant "work for hire" and came to denote the motivation of mercenary self-seeking; it appears in the vice lists of Galatians 5:20 and Philippians 2:3. Those characterized by it are contrasted with those who seek glory (v7) — the moral contrast is between self-seeking and God-seeking as the organizing principle of one's life.
9: Ἰουδαίῳ τε πρῶτον καὶ Ἕλληνι — the Jew-first priority in v16 (grace) is now applied to tribulation: covenant privilege brings covenant accountability. The prōton (first) is symmetrical — the primacy is in both directions, which is why Paul says it in both 1:16 and Romans 2:9-10.
10: δόξα δὲ καὶ τιμὴ καὶ εἰρήνη (doxa de kai timē kai eirēnē) — the three goods of v7 (glory, honor) are repeated with eirēnē (peace) substituted for aphtharsian (immortality); peace is the present-tense experience of what immortality will complete. The parallel structuring of vv9-10 by the repeated Ἰουδαίῳ τε πρῶτον καὶ Ἕλληνι reinforces the impartiality principle.
11: προσωπολημψία (prosōpolēmpsia) — "face-taking" — a Hebrew idiom (nasa panim = lift the face toward someone in favor) translated into Greek. The divine impartiality Paul asserts is a direct echo of Deuteronomy 10:17 ("the LORD your God does not show favoritism") applied now to Jewish-Gentile distinctions in divine judgment.
12: ἀνόμως ἥμαρτον, ἀνόμως καὶ ἀπολοῦνται (anomōs hēmarton, anomōs kai apolountai) — "sinned without law... will perish without law." The anōmos / en nomō contrast structures the principle of judgment by the available standard. Gentiles are not judged against a law they did not have; they are judged by what they did have (the natural law of vv14-15).
13: ποιηταὶ νόμου δικαιωθήσονται (poiētai nomou dikaiōthēsontai) — "doers of the law will be justified." The future passive of dikaioō is forensic: declared righteous at the eschatological judgment. Paul is stating the principle of Leviticus 18:5 seriously before showing in Romans 3:9-20 that no one is actually such a doer; the principle stands but the actuality refutes all claimants.
14: φύσει (physei) — "by nature" — a dative of manner, not dative of origin (not "by birth" but "by the nature of things"). Gentiles who do instinctively what the law requires demonstrate that natural moral knowledge exists independently of Sinai's revelation. ἑαυτοῖς εἰσιν νόμος (heautois eisin nomos) — "they are a law to themselves" — not moral relativism but the affirmation that the law's normative claim is written into human nature.
15: συνειδήσεως (syneidēseōs) — "conscience" — from syn-oida (to know together with); the conscience is the faculty of co-knowing, the internal witness that evaluates one's own actions by a shared moral standard. τῶν λογισμῶν κατηγορούντων ἢ καὶ ἀπολογουμένων — "the thoughts accusing or defending" — the forensic vocabulary (accuse/defend) is explicit: the conscience conducts an interior trial.
16: ἐν ᾗ ἡμέρᾳ (en hē hēmera) — "on the day when" — the relative clause connects back to v13's principle; the justification of v13 will occur at the eschatological judgment. διὰ Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ — "through Christ Jesus" — the divine judge of the secrets of humanity operates through the mediatorial agency of Christ, unexpected in the context of universal moral judgment but consistent with Paul's Christological totality.
17: Ἰουδαῖος ἐπονομάζῃ (Ioudaios eponomazē) — "you call yourself a Jew" — the present middle indicative; the self-designation is a claim being made repeatedly. From vv17-20 Paul lists five privileges the Jew claims: (1) the name/identity, (2) reliance on Torah, (3) boasting in God, (4) knowing God's will, (5) discerning what matters. The list sets up the irony of vv21-24.
18: δοκιμάζεις τὰ διαφέροντα (dokimazeis ta diapheronta) — "you approve/discern what matters" — dokimazō is the testing/assaying term (see Romans 1:28); ta diapheronta can mean "the things that differ" (distinguishing between options) or "the things that are superior" (preferring what matters). The ambiguity is likely intentional: the instruction of the law provides both distinction and prioritization.
19: ὁδηγὸν εἶναι τυφλῶν (hodēgon einai typhlōn) — "a guide to the blind" — hodēgos = road-guide (hodos + agō). The claim echoes Isaiah 42:6-7 (the Servant as a light to the blind and a covenant to the nations); Paul uses the self-designation of the Servant to describe the Jew's self-understanding, setting up the irony that the guide needs guiding.
20: μορφωσιν τῆς γνώσεως καὶ τῆς ἀληθείας (morphōsin tēs gnōseōs kai tēs alētheias) — "the form of knowledge and truth" — morphōsis denotes the external shape or appearance of something; Paul's choice of this word is already critical: the law gives the Jewish teacher an external form (morphōsis) of knowledge without necessarily the inner reality (morphē would be the real form).
21: κηρύσσων μὴ κλέπτειν κλέπτεις (kēryssōn mē kleptein klepteis) — "you who preach not to steal, do you steal?" — the present active participle (preaching) paired with the present indicative (stealing) creates the indictment of simultaneous proclamation and violation. The preacher-thief is not hypothetical but representative of a recognized pattern in Paul's indictment.
22: βδελυσσόμενος τὰ εἴδωλα ἱεροσυλεῖς (bdelyssomenos ta eidōla hierosyleis) — "you who abhor idols, do you rob temples?" — hierosyleis (to rob a temple) could refer to literal sacrilege (looting pagan shrines while professing to detest idolatry) or to the broader spiritual idolatry of covetousness Paul will address elsewhere. Either way, the verbal irony is sharp.
23: νόμου παράβασις (nomou parabasis) — "transgression of the law" — parabasis (stepping alongside, stepping past) is more deliberate than mere failure (hamartia); it implies knowing the boundary and crossing it intentionally. This is the specific accusation: not ignorance of the law but active violation of the law one claims to teach.
24: τὸ γὰρ ὄνομα τοῦ θεοῦ δι᾽ ὑμᾶς βλασφημεῖται — "for the name of God is blasphemed among the Gentiles because of you" — a quotation from LXX Isaiah 52:5 (cf. Ezekiel 36:20-22). The present passive blasphēmeitai indicates an ongoing reality, not a historical incident; the Jewish community's covenant violations have become a sustained cause of Gentile contempt for the God who claims them.
25: περιτομὴ μὲν γὰρ ὠφελεῖ (peritomē men gar ōphelei) — "circumcision benefits" — Paul affirms the real value of circumcision before qualifying it; the men gar construction acknowledges genuine advantage. ἐὰν νόμον πράσσῃς — "if you practice the law" — the conditional shifts the benefit from the rite itself to the covenantal obedience it signs; the sign without the signified reality is null.
26: ἡ ἀκροβυστία αὐτοῦ εἰς περιτομὴν λογισθήσεται (hē akrobystia autou eis peritomēn logisthēsetai) — "his uncircumcision will be reckoned as circumcision" — logizō again: the forensic reckoning that counts uncircumcision as circumcision when covenant faithfulness is present. Paul applies the same reckoning-logic he will use for Abraham's faith in Romans 4 to the Gentile who keeps the law's requirements.
27: κρινεῖ (krinei) — future active, "will judge" — the eschatological scenario: the obedient Gentile standing as a judge over the disobedient, law-possessing Jew. The reversal echoes the OT tradition of Nineveh's repentance condemning Israel (Matthew 12:41); external markers of covenant membership without covenant faithfulness cannot survive the judgment.
28: ἐν τῷ κρυπτῷ Ἰουδαῖος (en tō kryptō Ioudaios) — "in the hidden Jew" — kryptos (hidden, inward) contrasts with the external/visible (phaneros, v28). Paul's paradox: the real Jew is the one who is a Jew in the hidden, interior reality — a formulation that both affirms Jewish particularity and universalizes it by grounding Jewishness in the inward reality that the covenant was always aimed at (Deuteronomy 30:6 — circumcision of the heart).
29: περιτομὴ καρδίας ἐν πνεύματι οὐ γράμματι (peritomē kardias en pneumati ou grammati) — "circumcision of heart, in Spirit not letter" — the pneuma/gramma (spirit/letter) antithesis Paul will develop in 7:6 and 2 Corinthians 3:6. The circumcision the OT promises (Deuteronomy 30:6; Jeremiah 4:4; Ezekiel 36:26-27) is the interior reality of which the external rite is a sign. ἔπαινος οὐκ ἐξ ἀνθρώπων ἀλλ᾽ ἐκ τοῦ θεοῦ — praise from God rather than humans is the ultimate validation — a direct inversion of the honor-shame framework that makes human praise the measure of worth.
